old_claim.note: Public Archives provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Customs checkpoints required tax calculation and collection practices; Roman stationes checked merchandise and collected portorium as a transport/customs tax.

source_shape.source_locator: The introduction defines portorium as a transport tax and says customs offices, called stationes, checked merchandise and collected the tax.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Roman customs posts were tax-collection offices where merchandise was checked and portorium was collected.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old public-archives edge described a broad administrative backdrop; the source-backed mechanism is fiscal assessment and collection, which is represented more directly by tax_accounting.

ontology_before: Border Customs Posts depended on Currency and Public Archives, implying public record repositories were the direct administrative mechanism for customs checkpoints.
ontology_after: Border Customs Posts is scoped to customs checkpoints and stationes; currency supports valuation, while tax_accounting carries tariff calculation and collection.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: border_customs_posts now depends on tax_accounting and no longer directly depends on public_archives.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to archived customs-law tablets rather than checkpoint/tax-office operation.
A source shows the earliest scoped customs checkpoints functioned only as document archives without tariff calculation or collection.
A future customs_law_archives node is added and should take the public_archives dependency.
short_reason: Customs posts are tax-assessment and collection offices; public archives were broader than the direct mechanism.
